original_abstract:
This article provides a comprehensive review of the current state of the art in overlapping community detection, covering algorithms, quality metrics, and benchmarking toolsets. We conduct a rigorous comparative study of fourteen different algorithms to evaluate their effectiveness across various scenarios. Moving beyond standard community-level assessments, we introduce an evaluation framework specifically designed to measure an algorithm’s precision in identifying overlapping nodes. This approach allows for a deeper analysis of "over-detection" and "under-detection" tendencies at the node level.

Our findings, based on community-level metrics (Normalized Mutual Information and the Omega Index) and node-level accuracy (F-score), reveal several key insights. In networks characterized by low overlapping density, the SLPA, OSLOM, Game, and COPRA algorithms consistently outperform their competitors. In more complex environments featuring high overlapping density and diversity, SLPA and Game emerge as the most stable performers. However, our results also indicate that accurately resolving structures in these high-overlap networks remains an open challenge for the field. Finally, we observe a consistent trend across various algorithms when applied to real-world social networks: overlapping nodes typically constitute a relatively small fraction of the total population (often under 30%), with most of these nodes belonging to only two or three distinct communities.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract — Community detection in complex networks is a widely studied topic, with both theoretical and practical implications. Despite a large body of research that has been devoted to non-overlapping communities, there are many scenarios in which communities may be allowed to overlap. Overlapping community detection, or multi-community membership, is thus of increasing interest and the aim of this paper is to provide a detailed review of work in this field to date, reviewing algorithms, evaluation criteria and tools for benchmarking graph clustering algorithms in this context. Within this framework we conduct an in-depth comparison of 14 graph clustering algorithms to consider their performance under the different criteria defined above. In addition to the more common criteria such as modularity, conductance and clustering coefficient for evaluating non-overlapping community structures, we propose new criteria to estimate the accuracy of algorithms in identifying the number of overlapping nodes, which may reflect the amount of over- and under-detection of such nodes.

We analyze the performance of SLPA, OSLOM, Game, and COPRA on community detection using community-level metrics like NMIs and the Omega Index as well as node-level metrics like F-score. Our analysis reveals many interesting results. First of all, SLPA, OSLOM, Game and COPRA are mostly the winners in low overlapping density (asd) graphs. Second, SLPA and Game are the more stable algorithms in high overlapping density (asd) graphs. Finally, although all algorithms perform very well, there is a clear picture across different versions of all real world social networks we have access to: in almost all cases the fraction of overlapping nodes in the graph is less than 30% and most overlapping nodes are in exactly 2 or 3 communities.

We analyze the performance of SLPA, OSLOM, Game, and COPRA on community detection using community-level metrics like NMIs and the Omega Index as well as node-level metrics like F-score. Our analysis reveals many interesting results. First of all, SLPA, OSLOM, Game and COPRA are mostly the winners in low overlapping density (asd) graphs. Second, SLPA and Game are the more stable algorithms in high overlapping density (asd) graphs. document.input:
This article provides a comprehensive review of the current state of the art in overlapping community detection, covering algorithms, quality metrics, and benchmarking toolsets. We conduct a rigorous comparative study of fourteen different algorithms to evaluate their effectiveness across various scenarios. Moving beyond standard community-level assessments, we introduce an evaluation framework specifically designed to measure an algorithm’s precision in identifying overlapping nodes. This approach allows for a deeper analysis of "over-detection" and "under-detection" tendencies at the node level.

Our findings, based on community-level metrics (Normalized Mutual Information and the Omega Index) and node-level accuracy (F-score), reveal several key insights. In networks characterized by low overlapping density, the SLPA, OSLOM, Game, and COPRA algorithms consistently outperform their competitors. In more complex environments featuring high overlapping density and diversity, SLPA and Game emerge as the most stable performers. However, our results also indicate that accurately resolving structures in these high-overlap networks remains an open challenge for the field. Finally, we observe a consistent trend across various algorithms when applied to real-world social networks: overlapping nodes typically constitute a relatively small fraction of the total population (often under 30%), with most of these nodes belonging to only two or three distinct communities.
